era_context: The mid-8th century continued the pattern of post-Umayyad reorganization in the Islamic world, Tang Dynasty recovery from the An Lushan Rebellion, and Frankish consolidation in Western Europe. This was a period of administrative and military restructuring across major Eurasian powers, with limited documentation for most of the world outside these centers.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond the Frankish realm

events.title: Baghdad construction continues under al-Mansur
events.description: The construction of Baghdad, begun in 762 CE, continued under Caliph al-Mansur's direction. The city was designed as a circular, fortified capital with the caliph's palace at the center, representing a new model of Islamic urbanism and administrative centralization. By 764, the basic infrastructure was taking shape, though the city would not be fully completed until 766 CE. This project symbolized the Abbasid shift from Arab tribal governance to a centralized, Persian-influenced bureaucratic state.

sources.name: al-Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings
al-Khatib al-Baghdadi, History of Baghdad (Tarikh Baghdad)

events.certainty_note: The timeline of Baghdad's construction is well-attested in Islamic sources, though specific progress in 764 CE is not detailed. Archaeological evidence confirms the city's layout and construction phases.

events.title: Byzantine-Arab naval conflict in the Mediterranean
events.region: Eastern Mediterranean (Byzantine Empire vs. Umayyad/Abbasid remnants)
events.description: The Byzantine Empire under Constantine V (r. 741–775 CE) continued its naval campaigns against Arab forces in the Mediterranean. While the major Umayyad Caliphate had fallen in 750 CE, Arab naval power remained a significant threat. Byzantine sources record ongoing raids and counter-raids in the 760s, though specific engagements in 764 CE are not always precisely documented. These conflicts were part of the broader Byzantine-Arab struggle that had defined the 7th and 8th centuries.
events.certainty_note: Byzantine sources document Constantine V's military activities in the 760s, but specific events in 764 CE are not always clearly dated. The broader pattern of Byzantine-Arab conflict is well-attested.

events.title: Iconoclasm controversy in Byzantine Empire
events.description: The Iconoclastic Controversy, which had begun in the early 8th century, continued to dominate Byzantine religious and political life under Constantine V. Constantine V was a strong iconoclast (opponent of religious images), and his policies enforced the destruction of icons and suppression of their veneration. By 764, this had been the official policy for decades, creating significant tension with the monastic establishment and the papacy. The controversy would not be fully resolved until 843 CE.
events.certainty_note: The Iconoclastic Controversy is well-documented in both Byzantine and papal sources. Constantine V's iconoclastic policies are clearly attested for the 760s.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of Baghdad's construction is sometimes overstated as an immediate transformation of Islamic governance. In reality, the transition from Umayyad to Abbasid administrative models was gradual, and Baghdad's completion in 766 CE did not immediately resolve factional tensions within the caliphate. Additionally, the Iconoclastic Controversy is often presented as a clear ideological divide, but recent scholarship suggests it was more complex, involving political factionalism, monastic interests, and theological debate rather than a simple binary opposition.
historiographic_note: Sources for 764 CE are concentrated in the Islamic world and Byzantine Empire, where court historiography was well-developed. Islamic sources are often compiled centuries after events and reflect later theological perspectives. Byzantine sources are somewhat more contemporary but still subject to political bias. Western European sources are sparse and often legendary. The absence of documentation for most of the world outside these centers reflects the bias of surviving written records toward literate, centralized states.

graph_edges.note: Byzantine-Arab conflict continued after the fall of the Umayyad Caliphate
